content.text: Reviewing code that integrates an LLM agent into an application requires a different lens than reviewing standard backend code. Agent integration code is the boundary layer between deterministic application logic and non-deterministic model behavior. Errors at this boundary are uniquely dangerous: a missing output validation can cause unhandled model responses to propagate through the system; an unsanitized user input passed directly to a tool call can enable injection attacks; a missing timeout can cause cascading failures under load. A structured code review checklist ensures reviewers examine agent-specific concerns systematically rather than relying on general engineering intuition.
What it is

A code review checklist for agent integrations is a structured set of review criteria organized by concern category — tool schema design, prompt construction, action safety, observability, and error handling — that reviewers apply when evaluating pull requests that add or modify code connecting application logic to LLM agents. Unlike a general code quality checklist, it addresses concerns that are specific to the non-deterministic, multi-step nature of agent systems: does the code handle cases where the model returns malformed output? does it bound agent action scope? does it propagate trace context through the agent call graph?

The checklist is codified in the repository and referenced in the PR template so that reviewers cannot forget to apply it. It is maintained by the AI platform team and updated as the team discovers new failure modes in production or through adversarial testing. Each checklist item is binary: the reviewer marks it as passing, failing, or not-applicable for the specific PR being reviewed, and records a comment explaining any failing item.
Why it matters

Agent integration code has a higher failure surface than typical application code because it involves three interacting non-deterministic components: the LLM itself (whose outputs vary across calls), the tool execution layer (which may interact with external services), and the orchestration logic (which drives multi-turn decision loops). Standard code review focuses on logic correctness, type safety, and test coverage. These are necessary but not sufficient for agent code: a correctly typed function that passes the wrong context to a model can cause arbitrarily wrong agent behavior that no static analysis will detect.

The checklist provides a concrete vocabulary for reviewers to express concerns about agent-specific failure modes. Without it, reviewers may approve code that looks correct from a general engineering perspective but has critical gaps — no rate limiting on tool calls, no validation of structured outputs, no cost circuit breaker — that only manifest under production conditions. The checklist bridges the gap between general engineering expertise and agent-specific domain knowledge.
Benefits
Systematic coverage: every PR touching agent code is evaluated against the same set of criteria, preventing important checks from being accidentally omitted.
Onboarding acceleration: new engineers learn agent-specific review concerns by applying a concrete checklist rather than discovering them through trial and error in production.
Actionable feedback: checklist-anchored comments are easier to resolve than open-ended review feedback because the author knows exactly which criterion needs to be addressed.
Pattern detection across PRs: when the same checklist item fails repeatedly, the team can identify systematic gaps in training, tooling, or architecture that need to be addressed upstream.
Shared vocabulary: the checklist creates common terminology for agent-specific concerns that facilitates clear communication between engineers, safety reviewers, and SREs.
Compliance evidence: a completed checklist attached to each PR provides documentation that agent-specific due diligence was performed before merge.
Trade-offs and limitations
Checklist fatigue: long checklists applied uniformly to all PRs can become mechanical tick-box exercises; reviewers mark items as passing without genuinely evaluating them.
False coverage: a checklist cannot cover novel failure modes not yet represented in its items; new agent capabilities may introduce risks the checklist does not address until it is updated.
Context dependency: some checklist items (such as 'action scope is appropriate') require contextual judgment that a binary pass/fail format may not adequately capture.
Maintenance overhead: the checklist must be actively maintained as the platform evolves; a stale checklist that references outdated patterns creates reviewer confusion.
Scope creep: without disciplined curation, checklists tend to grow over time as items are added for each new failure mode but rarely removed, eventually becoming unwieldy.
How it works

When a developer opens a PR that touches agent integration code (detected by file path patterns matching src/agents/**, src/tools/**, or similar), the CI system automatically attaches the current checklist to the PR as a comment. The checklist is organized into sections, each containing two to five specific questions. Reviewers read the diff against each section, evaluate whether the code satisfies the criterion, and leave a review comment referencing the specific checklist item for any failures.

The review process requires at least one engineer familiar with agent systems to complete the checklist. For high-risk changes (new tool integrations, changes to the action execution layer, modifications to context management), a second reviewer from the AI platform team provides a focused review of the action safety and observability sections. The PR template includes a checkbox that the author completes as a self-review before requesting human review, surfacing any items the author is uncertain about so reviewers can allocate attention appropriately.

After all checklist items are addressed, the reviewer posts an approval. Checklist completion is tracked over time: unresolved items at merge time are logged as known issues with an owner and a resolution date, preventing checklist failures from silently blocking merge while avoiding indefinite blocking on minor items that can be addressed in follow-up PRs.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Code review checklist flow: PR opened, CI checks, safety scan, human review with checklist, merge

content.text: The diagram shows the four-stage PR review flow for agent integration code. Automated CI checks and a safety scan complete first, narrowing the surface area for human review. The human reviewer applies the checklist across four concern categories: tool schema, prompt handling, action safety, and observability. The outcome is one of three states: approve, request changes with specific checklist citations, or reject with escalation to the safety team.
When to use it

The checklist applies to any PR that modifies code in the agent integration layer: tool definitions, tool execution wrappers, agent orchestration logic, prompt construction code, model call sites, output parsing and validation, memory or context management, and agent configuration files. It does not apply to purely cosmetic changes, documentation-only PRs, or test-only changes that do not affect the production agent code path.

For experimental feature branches where code is not intended to reach production, a lightweight version of the checklist covering only security and data-handling concerns may be appropriate. The full checklist should always apply before code is promoted from a feature branch to a release branch.
How to implement

Catalog the agent-specific failure modes observed in your system to date, including near-misses and production incidents, to populate the initial checklist with real risks rather than hypothetical ones.
Organize checklist items into categories (tool schema, prompt construction, action safety, observability, error handling) with no more than five items per category to keep the checklist scannable.
Add the checklist to the repository as a markdown file and reference it in the PR template so that every agent-integration PR includes a checklist link.
Configure a CI check or GitHub App that automatically comments the checklist on PRs matching agent-code file patterns.
Define what constitutes a blocking vs. advisory checklist item and document the criteria so reviewers apply them consistently.
Require at least one reviewer with agent-systems expertise to complete the checklist; use CODEOWNERS to enforce this automatically.
Review and update the checklist quarterly, adding items for newly discovered failure modes and removing or merging items that have become redundant.
Track checklist completion metrics (rate of items marked as failing, most commonly failed items) to identify systematic gaps in the codebase.
Examples

## Agent Integration Review Checklist

### Tool Schema
- [ ] All tool input parameters are validated before use; malformed inputs are rejected with a clear error, not silently coerced
- [ ] Tool output types are narrowly specified; the code handles the case where the model returns a response that does not match the expected schema
- [ ] Tool descriptions accurately represent what the tool does; no misleading capability claims that could cause the model to misuse the tool
- [ ] Each tool has an explicit timeout; long-running tools use async patterns and do not block the agent event loop

### Prompt Construction
- [ ] User-supplied content is not interpolated directly into prompt strings; it is passed as a structured input or sanitized before inclusion
- [ ] The prompt does not include credentials, PII, or internal system details that should not be exposed to the model
- [ ] Context window usage is bounded; the code does not pass unbounded user-supplied context that could exceed the model's limit
- [ ] Few-shot examples (if any) are stored in version-controlled files, not hardcoded inline

### Action Safety
- [ ] The agent cannot take irreversible actions (delete, send, publish) without explicit confirmation or a human-in-the-loop gate
- [ ] Each external action call is idempotent, or the code handles duplicate execution gracefully
- [ ] The action scope matches the agent's stated purpose; the code does not grant access to systems beyond what the agent's task requires
- [ ] Rate limiting is applied to tool calls that interact with external APIs; the agent cannot run unconstrained loops against paid APIs

### Observability
- [ ] Every model call logs the prompt hash, model version, token counts (input + output), latency, and cost
- [ ] Trace IDs are propagated from the request context through all model calls and tool executions
- [ ] Agent execution paths that could enter infinite or excessively long loops have explicit iteration limits and abort conditions
- [ ] Eval assertions exist for the new agent behavior being added; the test suite covers at least the happy path and one failure mode
Real-world example

A logistics company built an agent that processed customer shipment queries by calling a carrier API tool. The initial implementation passed the full user message directly to the tool call without validation. During review, the checklist item 'User-supplied content is not interpolated directly into prompt strings' flagged this pattern. The reviewer also noted that the carrier API tool had no timeout, violating the tool schema checklist item requiring explicit timeouts.

Both issues were fixed before merge: the tool input was validated against a strict schema that extracted only the shipment tracking number, and a 10-second timeout was added to the carrier API call with a fallback message if the API did not respond. Six weeks later, a carrier API outage that had previously caused cascading failures was gracefully handled due to the timeout — the agent returned an informative error message instead of hanging. The validation change also prevented a customer from injecting a specially crafted tracking number that attempted to elicit non-standard API behavior.
Common mistakes
Treating the checklist as a formality and marking all items as passing without reading the diff carefully against each criterion.
Adding checklist items reactively after each incident without removing stale items, causing the checklist to grow beyond reviewers' attention spans.
Assigning agent integration review to engineers without agent-systems experience who cannot meaningfully evaluate checklist items about action safety or context window management.
Not enforcing the checklist via CODEOWNERS, leaving it as an optional tool that gets skipped under delivery pressure.
Using the checklist only for new features but not for refactors, bug fixes, or dependency upgrades that modify agent integration code.
Failing to update the checklist when new tools or agent capabilities are introduced, leaving coverage gaps for the newest and highest-risk code.
Best practices
Keep the checklist short: aim for 20-25 items across all categories; longer checklists are skimmed rather than read.
Anchor each item to a specific failure mode with a brief explanation of why it matters, so reviewers understand the intent rather than mechanically applying a rule.
Treat the checklist as living documentation: track which items are most frequently failed and use that data to prioritize architectural improvements that make those items pass by default.
Pair the checklist with a shared library of anti-patterns — code snippets that illustrate what a failing item looks like — so reviewers have concrete comparisons.
Automate as many checklist items as possible via static analysis, type checking, or linting rules so human review focuses on judgment-requiring items.
Run a quarterly calibration session where the team reviews a set of real PRs together and discusses how each checklist item was applied to develop shared standards.
Performance and scalability

The checklist adds review time proportional to its length and the complexity of the PR. A well-maintained 20-item checklist takes an experienced reviewer approximately 15-30 minutes to apply to a medium-complexity agent integration PR. This overhead is justified by the defect prevention value, but it must be managed: applying the full checklist to every trivial change creates reviewer burnout.

At scale, automate all statically checkable items. A linter that detects direct user input interpolation into prompts, a type checker that enforces output schema validation, and a CI check that verifies trace ID propagation reduce the human reviewer's checklist to the judgment-requiring items that automation cannot evaluate. As automation coverage grows, the human checklist shrinks, and reviewer time is concentrated where human judgment is genuinely needed.

Security and reliability

The most critical security items in the checklist are prompt injection prevention (user input not directly interpolated into prompts) and action scope validation (agent cannot exceed its intended permissions). These two items, if failed and merged, can create exploitable vulnerabilities in production. The checklist makes these concerns visible to reviewers who might otherwise focus on functional correctness and miss them.

From a reliability perspective, the timeout and rate limiting checklist items prevent agent code from becoming a source of cascading failures. An agent that calls an external tool in an unbounded retry loop or without a timeout can exhaust connection pools, trigger API rate limits on shared credentials, or stall entire request processing pipelines. The checklist catches these patterns before they reach production.
Checklist item lifecycle and governance

Each checklist item has a lifecycle: proposed, pilot, standard, deprecated. A new item enters as 'pilot' when a failure mode is first observed. During a pilot period of one to two sprints, reviewers apply the item and document how often it fires, whether it catches real issues or generates false positives, and whether it can be automated. Items that consistently catch real issues and are not automatable become 'standard'. Items that generate too many false positives or are superseded by automation are deprecated and removed.

Governance of the checklist lives with the AI platform team, which reviews proposed additions from any team member and makes final decisions on inclusion. This prevents the checklist from becoming a wish list that every team member adds items to without corresponding removals. The governance process also ensures consistency: if two teams propose items addressing the same failure mode, they are merged into a single item with unified wording rather than added as two separate overlapping items.
Self-review by PR authors

Requiring authors to complete the checklist as a self-review before requesting human review has two benefits: it catches obvious issues before the reviewer engages (reducing back-and-forth), and it builds checklist internalization in authors over time so they write code that passes the checklist by default. The PR template includes an author self-review section where the author marks each item, flags uncertain items with a comment, and explains any items they believe are not applicable to their change.

An author who marks an item as not applicable must include a brief justification. This prevents not-applicable from becoming a way to skip difficult items. Common legitimate not-applicable cases include: a PR that only modifies an agent's output formatting does not need to address action scope (action-safety items are not applicable), or a PR that adds a read-only data retrieval tool does not need to address rollback paths (the action is inherently safe). These justifications are reviewed by the human reviewer along with the checklist.
Connecting checklist to architecture decisions

Many checklist items represent architectural concerns that should be addressed at the system level rather than patched in individual PRs. When a checklist item fails on three or more PRs in a quarter, the AI platform team raises an architecture improvement initiative to address the root cause. For example, if 'trace IDs are propagated through all model calls' fails repeatedly, the team adds trace propagation to the shared agent base class so that all future agent implementations inherit correct tracing by default, eliminating the need for each PR to implement it individually.

This feedback loop between checklist results and architectural investment is one of the most valuable outcomes of maintaining a structured checklist over time. The checklist becomes a sensor for systemic platform gaps, and the platform team uses checklist failure data to prioritize shared tooling improvements that raise the floor for all agent integrations rather than relying on each team to get every item right individually.
Integrating checklist findings into retrospectives

Checklist data should flow into engineering retrospectives. At the end of each sprint, the team reviews how many PRs were reviewed with the checklist, which items were most commonly failed, and whether any failures resulted in blocked merges or follow-up tickets. This review serves two purposes: it keeps the checklist visible as a tool the team genuinely uses rather than a bureaucratic artifact, and it surfaces patterns that indicate whether the team's agent engineering practices are improving over time.

When a checklist item fails in a PR review and the same issue is found in production a week later, that pair of events is documented as a validation data point for the checklist's value. Conversely, when items consistently pass without catching any issues for a sustained period, the team evaluates whether the underlying risk has been eliminated by an architectural change, in which case the item can be retired, or whether the item is too vague for reviewers to evaluate meaningfully, in which case it needs to be rewritten with a concrete criterion. Both directions of feedback keep the checklist sharp and relevant.
Tooling automation for checklist enforcement

Several checklist items can be enforced automatically with existing tools rather than relying on reviewer memory. A Semgrep ruleset can detect patterns like f-string interpolation of request parameters into prompt strings, missing timeout parameters in HTTP client calls within tool implementations, and direct use of raw model output without parsing. A mypy strict mode configuration can catch missing return type annotations on tool call functions, which often indicates that the author has not considered the full range of possible outputs. A custom pre-commit hook can verify that every new agent class imports from the shared base class that provides built-in trace propagation and cost logging.

The goal is to automate every checklist item that has a deterministic definition — those that can be expressed as 'the code must contain X' or 'the code must not contain Y'. Judgment-requiring items that depend on understanding intent — 'the action scope matches the agent's stated purpose' or 'the tool description accurately represents what the tool does' — must remain with the human reviewer. As the automation coverage grows, the human review checklist shrinks to its irreducible judgment core, making reviewer time more focused and valuable.

When a new checklist item is added, the AI platform team immediately investigates whether it can be automated. If a Semgrep rule or type annotation convention can catch the failure mode, the rule is implemented and the checklist item becomes an automation rather than a human check. This prevents the human checklist from growing indefinitely as new failure modes are discovered: new risks get automated gates, and the human checklist stays bounded.
Key takeaways
Agent integration code requires a specialized review checklist because standard code review criteria do not cover the non-deterministic failure modes specific to LLM agents.
The checklist should be organized by concern category (tool schema, prompt construction, action safety, observability) with no more than 25 items total.
Automate all statically verifiable checklist items to concentrate human review on judgment-requiring concerns.
Anchor each item to a real failure mode with a brief rationale so reviewers understand the intent and can apply the criterion correctly.
Maintain the checklist as living documentation: update quarterly, add items for new failure modes, and remove stale items.
Require at least one agent-systems-experienced reviewer for all agent integration PRs; CODEOWNERS enforces this automatically.
